title: Add analog simulation analyses and parameter sweep RFC
description: Add dedicated analog.simulation elements for transient, DC operating point, direct DC sweep, and AC sweep usage, along with one-dimensional component sweeps through a nested analog.sweepparameter with parameter-specific target props, defining the Circuit JSON experiments, sweep relationships, and analysis-specific voltagecurrent result types produced by that TSX.
body:
## Summary

- add dedicated `<analog.*simulation>` elements for transient, DC operating point, direct DC sweep, and AC sweep usage
- add one-dimensional component sweeps through a nested `<analog.sweepparameter>` with parameter-specific target props
- define the Circuit JSON experiments, sweep relationships, and analysis-specific voltage/current result types produced by that TSX
- preserve the existing `<analogsimulation>` transient spelling and transient graph elements

## Scope

This RFC is intentionally limited to TSX usage and the corresponding Circuit JSON. Compiler, engine, scheduling, rendering, export, measurement-expression, and implementation-order contracts are outside its scope.

## Validation

- parsed all embedded JSON examples
- `git diff --check`

title: Add FDM enclosure solver dependency
description: Adds the tscircuitcreate-fdm-enclosure dependency to the aggregate package, allowing the package updater to complete its dependency synchronization without failure.
body:
## Summary

- add `@tscircuit/create-fdm-enclosure@0.0.2` to the aggregate package dependencies
- replay the package versions from the updater run that was blocked by the missing dependency
- update `bun.lock` with the enclosure solver and its transitive dependencies

## Root cause

After `@tscircuit/core` added the enclosure solver as a peer dependency, `scripts/copy-core-versions.ts` detected that the aggregate `tscircuit` package did not declare it and threw:

```
Missing core dependencies in package.json: @tscircuit/create-fdm-enclosure
```

This caused the two latest **Update @tscircuit Package** workflow runs to fail before they could create their dependency-update PR.

## Impact

The aggregate package now supplies core's enclosure solver peer dependency. The package updater can complete its dependency synchronization instead of failing during `copy-core-versions`.

## Validation

- reproduced the exact failed workflow step:
  - `bun update --latest @tscircuit/core @tscircuit/cli @tscircuit/eval @tscircuit/runframe`
  - `bun run copy-core-versions`
- `bun install --frozen-lockfile --ignore-scripts`
- `bun run build`
- `bun test` — 3 passing

title: Add schematic component overlap warning
description: Add a schematic component overlap warning to identify overlapping schematic components and include it in AnyCircuitElement and generated reference docs.
body:
## Summary

- add a `schematic_component_overlap_warning` Circuit JSON element
- identify both overlapping schematic components and their optional sheet
- include the warning in `AnyCircuitElement` and generated reference docs

## Why

Core currently has no schema-valid warning type for reporting schematic component collisions. This is the Circuit JSON prerequisite for a Core design-rule check that detects positive-area overlap between rendered component bounds.

## Impact

This is additive. Existing Circuit JSON remains unchanged, while producers can emit a structured, non-fatal warning for overlapping schematic components.

## Validation

- `bun test`
- `bunx tsc --noEmit`
- `bun run check-snake-case`
- `bun run lint:zod`
- `bun run build`

title: Add disabled flags to project config
description: Add pcbDisabled and schematicDisabled flags to the exported ProjectConfig type, allowing consumers to avoid redeclaring these fields and providing a shared source of truth for JSON project configuration.
body:
## Summary

- add `pcbDisabled` and `schematicDisabled` to the exported `ProjectConfig` type
- include both flags in the `projectConfig` Zod schema
- add parser coverage for true and false values
- regenerate the checked-in README documentation

## Why

Consumers that load `tscircuit.config.json` currently need to redeclare these fields locally even though both already exist on `PlatformConfig`. Exposing them through `ProjectConfig` gives JSON project configuration a shared source of truth.

## Impact

Packages such as `@tscircuit/eval` can extend `ProjectConfig` and consume the rendering-disable flags without duplicating their definitions.

## Validation

- `bun test tests` (374 passing)
- `bun run typecheck`
- `bun run format:check`
- `bun run build`
- all repository-required generation scripts

title: Add schematic box chip reference props
description: Add name, chipRef, pinLabels, and schPinArrangement to schematicBoxProps and SchematicBoxProps, enabling better representation of chips across schematic sheets.
body:
## Summary

- add `name`, `chipRef`, `pinLabels`, and `schPinArrangement` to `schematicBoxProps` and `SchematicBoxProps`
- reuse the shared chip pin-label and schematic pin-arrangement validators
- add a regression test based on the multi-sheet chip example in tscircuit/core#2750
- regenerate the props documentation

## Why

[tscircuit/core#2750](https://github.com/tscircuit/core/pull/2750) uses multiple `<schematicbox />` elements to represent one chip across schematic sheets. The runtime schema and TypeScript interface did not declare the identity, chip linkage, pin-label, or pin-arrangement fields, so those props were rejected by TypeScript and stripped during parsing.

This change makes those fields part of the canonical schematic-box props API and preserves the shared pin-arrangement normalization behavior.

## Validation

- `bun test` (373 passing)
- `bun run typecheck`
- `bun run build`
- `bun run format:check`
- `bun run check-circular-deps`

title: Add pill pads to DFN footprints
description: Adds support for stadium-shaped copper pads in DFN footprints by modifying the DFN generator to honor the inherited pillpads parameter and exposing it in the typed DFN builder.
body:
## What changed

- make the DFN generator honor the inherited `pillpads` parameter
- expose `.pillpads(...)` on the typed DFN builder
- verify pad shape and radius with an SVG visual snapshot

## Why

Several imported JLCPCB DFN packages use stadium-shaped copper pads. The DFN schema already accepted `pillpads`, but the generator always emitted rectangular pads and the typed builder did not expose the option.

This is the footprinter prerequisite for exact matches such as BME280, U3018, PC400J00000F, SN74LVC1G123DCTR, and TC7WH157FU. A follow-up `circuit-json-to-footprinter` change is still needed to prioritize shape-matching seeds within its optimization cap; the standalone 250-board benchmark therefore remains 133/250 with the current matcher.

## Validation

- `bun test` — 451 passing
- `bun run build`
- `bunx biome format src/fn/dfn.ts src/footprinter.ts tests/dfn.test.ts`
- visually inspected `dfn8_pillpads.snap.svg`
- sequential 250-board jlc5000 benchmark — 133/250, zero errors with the current matcher

title: Add pill pads to quad footprints
description: Adds a _pillpads option to the quad generator, allowing for pill-shaped perimeter pads while maintaining rectangular thermal pads, improving the representation of imported EasyEDAJLC quad footprints.
body:
## What

- add a `_pillpads` option to the shared quad generator and expose it through QFN, QFP, TQFP, LQFP, MLP, and quad builders
- emit pill-shaped perimeter pads while keeping QFN thermal pads rectangular
- calculate pill radius from the shorter pad dimension so vertical and horizontal pads are both valid

## Why

Imported EasyEDA/JLC quad footprints frequently use pill-shaped copper pads. The quad generator could only emit rectangles, so `circuit-json-to-footprinter` could optimize the dimensions but could not represent the source geometry closely enough to cross the 98% IoU benchmark threshold.

## Impact

Tested with tscircuit/circuit-json-to-footprinter#16 against the 250-board tscircuit/jlc5000 corpus:

- published footprinter control: 91/250 at or above 98% IoU (36.4%)
- this branch: 99/250 (39.6%)
- eight additional boards cross the threshold, all selecting `qfn..._pillpads` strings
- combined with the matcher PR, the currently published 85/250 baseline rises to 99/250

## Checks

- `bun test` (450 pass, 0 fail)
- `bun run build`
- `bunx biome check src/fn/quad.ts src/helpers/pillpad.ts src/footprinter.ts tests/qfn.test.ts`
- `git diff --check`

title: Prefer readable pin names for anonymous schematic nets
description: Replaces generated slash-joined schematic net labels with readable component-pin labels, improving schematic readability and maintaining explicit trace labels.
body:
## Summary

- replace generated slash-joined schematic net labels such as `A/B/C` with one readable component-pin label
- choose a pin from the connected component with the highest pin count, using the lower component name and lower pin number as deterministic tie-breakers
- apply the fallback consistently to explicit traces, component `connections`, and source-port fallbacks
- preserve explicit trace labels and named nets such as `GND` and `VCC`
- refresh affected schematic and simulation snapshots

## Why

Slash-joined anonymous labels can become extremely long and make larger schematics difficult to read. A stable label such as `U1_X1` keeps the schematic compact while still identifying the net by a useful endpoint.

## Validation

- `bun run build`
- 33 affected schematic and simulation snapshot tests pass
- focused explicit-label, named-net, lower-pin, and `connections` regressions pass
- formatting and diff checks pass

title: Add repro for empty USB-C schematic after Parts Engine failure
description: Adds a reproduction test for a USB-C connector that fails to fetch circuit JSON from the Parts Engine, resulting in an empty schematic box.
body:
## Summary

- adds a focused reproduction for a USB-C connector whose Parts Engine circuit JSON fetch fails
- asserts that the failure is surfaced as a `source_part_not_found_warning`
- snapshots the current broken schematic output: a USB-C box with no pins

## Context

A standard USB-C connector currently derives its schematic ports from the fetched part circuit JSON. When the Parts Engine fetch fails, the connector is left with no ports and renders as an empty box.

This PR intentionally captures the existing behavior without fixing it so the fallback behavior can be addressed in a follow-up.

## Test plan

```sh
bun test tests/repros/repro-usbc-parts-engine-failure-empty-schematic.test.tsx
```

The test passes.

title: Render chip ports on internal circuit symbols
description: Allows chip schematic ports to be rendered on explicitly mapped internal circuit terminals, ensuring proper schematic representation without altering chip naming or geometry.
body:
## Summary

- update `circuit-json` to `^0.0.451`
- let `Port` place a chip schematic port on the explicitly mapped internal-circuit terminal
- emit separate internal and overlapping chip `schematic_port` records using the new Circuit JSON flags
- keep internal mapping connections out of rendered schematic traces while preserving external routing and named-net labels
- reject ambiguous cases where one chip port maps to multiple internal terminals

## Why

When `schShowInternalCircuit` replaces the chip box with internal symbols, the physical chip port still needs its own schematic representation so external selectors such as `U1.pin1` have a routable endpoint. The endpoint should live on the internal symbol without changing chip naming or using geometry to infer connectivity.

The implementation keeps this responsibility in `Port`. It does not modify `Chip`, override render phases, use declaration order, or perform geometric matching.

## Validation

- `bun test tests/features/internal-circuit/internal-circuit-ambiguous-chip-port-mapping.test.tsx tests/features/internal-circuit/internal-circuit-external-resistor-connection.test.tsx tests/repros/repro-internal-circuit-dual-mosfet.test.tsx tests/repros/repro-internal-circuit-hidden-by-default.test.tsx tests/repros/repro-internal-circuit-mixed-components.test.tsx tests/repros/repro-internal-circuit-package-scoping.test.tsx`
- `bun test tests/components/normal-components/chip.test.tsx tests/components/normal-components/chip-connections.test.tsx tests/components/normal-components/chip-port-schematic-position.test.tsx tests/components/normal-components/chip-traces.test.tsx tests/components/primitive-components/port-sch-stem-length.test.tsx tests/components/primitive-components/port-sch-stem-length-connected.test.tsx tests/components/normal-components/chip-with-subcircuit-net-label.test.tsx`

title: Update Node-compatible FDM enclosure package
description: Updates the tscircuitcreate-fdm-enclosure package from version 0.0.2 to 0.0.3 to resolve import issues in Node environments by providing compiled JavaScript and declarations instead of raw TypeScript files.
body:
## Summary

Update `@tscircuit/create-fdm-enclosure` from `0.0.2` to `0.0.3`.

## Why

Version `0.0.2` published its runtime entrypoint as raw TypeScript (`lib/index.ts`). Plain Node consumers fail to import core with `ERR_UNKNOWN_FILE_EXTENSION`, which caused `svg.tscircuit.com` render requests to return HTTP 500 after the FDM enclosure dependency was introduced.

Version `0.0.3`, published from tscircuit/create-fdm-enclosure#3, ships compiled JavaScript and declarations from `dist` with Node-compatible package exports.

## Validation

- `bun test tests/fiber/namespaced-enclosure-fdm-box.test.tsx tests/fiber/namespaced-enclosure-fdm-box-type.test.tsx tests/enclosure/enclosure-fdm-box-usb-cutout.test.tsx --timeout 120000` — 3 passed
- `bunx tsc --noEmit`
- `bun run build`
- imported the built `@tscircuit/core` package under plain Node and verified `RootCircuit` is available

title: Update enclosure snapshots for opaque PoppyGL rendering
description: Updates the enclosure snapshots to use PoppyGL 0.0.26, ensuring that the rendered images display fully opaque pixels instead of translucent ones.
body:
## Summary

- require `poppygl@^0.0.26` for 3D snapshot tests
- regenerate the angled and top-down FDM enclosure snapshots with the merged glTF alpha-mode fix
- replace the half-transparent enclosure pixels with fully opaque output

## Root cause

The enclosure snapshots were generated with PoppyGL 0.0.25. That renderer wrote `baseColorFactor[3]` into the PNG even when the glTF material used `alphaMode: "OPAQUE"`, leaving the enclosure pixels at alpha 127/255. PoppyGL 0.0.26 honors the alpha mode and writes opaque pixels.

## Impact

The enclosure snapshots no longer display a checkerboard/translucent wall region in GitHub's PNG viewer. Both updated PNGs have an alpha range of 255–255.

[tscircuit/tscircuit#4036](https://github.com/tscircuit/tscircuit/pull/4036) should merge first so this core release's downstream package update is not blocked by the newly required enclosure solver.

## Validation

- `bun test tests/enclosure/enclosure-fdm-box-usb-cutout.test.tsx`
- `bunx tsc --noEmit`
- `bun run build`
- ImageMagick alpha inspection reports `opaque=True`, `alpha-min=1`, and `alpha-max=1` for both snapshots

title: Generate namespaced FDM enclosures
description: Adds namespaced TSX elements for FDM enclosures, including the construction of a solver for generating enclosures based on PCB components and their apertures, along with 3D rendering capabilities.
body:
## Summary

- adds `<enclosure.fdm.Box boardRef="..." />` and `<enclosure.cutoutaperture />` namespaced TSX elements
- constructs `CreateFdmEnclosureSolver` from the referenced board and component-owned apertures
- registers the solver in core and emits `solver:started` with reconstructable constructor input for the standard solver debugger
- emits an opaque JSCAD-backed `cad_component` positioned below the board
- adds angled and top-down orthographic 3D snapshots using a ModelCDN USB-C connector

## Placement behavior

Each aperture owns the conversion from its nearest PCB component into the solver input. Its nearest enclosure wall and offset come from the component's cable insertion center when available. The aperture center is placed above the PCB using the floor thickness, board thickness, aperture dimensions, and margin.

## Package

Uses `@tscircuit/create-fdm-enclosure@0.0.2`, published through that repository's standard `bun-pver-release.yml` workflow.

## Validation

- `bunx biome check` on the changed TypeScript files
- `bunx tsc --noEmit`
- `bun run build`
- `bunx @tscircuit/dependency-check`
- `bun test tests/fiber/namespaced-enclosure-fdm-box.test.tsx tests/fiber/namespaced-enclosure-fdm-box-type.test.tsx tests/enclosure/enclosure-fdm-box-usb-cutout.test.tsx --timeout 120000`
- `git diff --check`

title: Refactor SchematicBox organization and Circuit JSON ID types
description: Moves the SchematicBox primitive into its own directory and updates its public export, derives local ID aliases from canonical Circuit JSON interfaces, renames mappings for clarity, and prepares layout trees for multiple schematic representations without changing existing runtime behavior.
body:
## Summary

- Move the `SchematicBox` primitive into its own `/SchematicBox` directory and update its public export.
- Derive local `SourceComponentId`, `SourceGroupId`, and `SchematicSheetId` aliases from canonical Circuit JSON interfaces where the mappings are defined.
- Rename schematic sheet and source group mappings so their key/value roles are explicit.
- Return layout trees with their typed schematic sheet ID and pass it through `applySchematicMatchPackLayoutToTree`.
- Scope schematic component lookup by source component and schematic sheet when a sheet ID is available.

## Why

This is a behavior-neutral prerequisite for the multi-sheet chip representation work. Landing the organization, naming, typing, and layout API plumbing separately keeps the follow-up feature PR focused on creating and routing the additional chip representations.

## Impact

No runtime behavior changes are intended for existing circuits. A source component currently has one schematic representation per layout tree, while the new optional sheet context prepares MatchPack for multiple representations.

## Validation

- `bun test tests/features/schematic-sheet`
- `bunx tsc --noEmit`

title: Add TFT display driver catalog page
description: Add a new tft_display_driverslist page and JSON endpoint to classify in-stock TFT support ICs as display controllers, biaspower ICs, gamma buffers, or backlight drivers, with filtering options.
body:
## Summary

- add a new `/tft_display_drivers/list` page and JSON endpoint
- classify in-stock TFT support ICs as display controllers, bias/power ICs, gamma buffers, or backlight drivers
- add package, driver type, basic-part, and preferred-part filters
- exclude segment-only LCD driver families such as HT1621
- add route-selection, classification, query, mapping, and rendering coverage

## Data selection

JLCPCB's catalog does not expose a dedicated TFT-driver subcategory. The route therefore starts with the existing `LCD Drivers` and `LED Drivers` catalog groups, then selects curated TFT-oriented manufacturer family prefixes. The current production catalog includes matching parts such as SSD1963 controllers, TPS651/AW375 bias ICs, BUF168 gamma buffers, and AP3041/AP5727 backlight drivers.

No derived-table rebuild is required; the page reads directly from `component_catalog`.

## Validation

- `bun test` — 144 passed
- `bunx tsc --noEmit`
- `bunx tsc --noEmit --project cf-proxy/tsconfig.json`
- `bun run format:check`
- `git diff --check`

title: Fix LCD display list and add LCD driver page
description: Fixes the LCD display derived table population and adds a new page for listing LCD drivers with filtering options.
body:
## Summary

- populate the LCD display derived table from current and historical LCD display subcategories
- add a separate `/lcd_drivers/list` page backed by the existing component catalog
- support package, Basic, and Preferred filters on the LCD driver page
- show LCD driver pricing, stock, and catalog attributes
- add regression coverage for candidate selection, driver queries, filters, and rendering

## Root cause

The LCD display derived-table builder selected candidates with a `description LIKE "%LCD%"` predicate. Synced catalog descriptions are now blank, so rebuilds produced an empty `lcd_display` table even though LCD modules remain available under dedicated subcategories.

LCD driver ICs are intentionally kept separate from display modules. The new driver route selects the existing `LCD Drivers` catalog subcategory directly, avoiding a second derived table and excluding unrelated display hardware.

## Impact

- after rebuilding and syncing `lcd_display`, `/lcd_display/list` and its JSON endpoint will return LCD modules again
- after deploying the worker, `/lcd_drivers/list` and `/lcd_drivers/list.json` will list in-stock LCD driver chips without an additional database rebuild

## Validation

- `bun test` — 141 tests passed
- targeted LCD driver route and rendering tests — 6 tests passed
- `bunx tsc --noEmit`
- `bunx tsc --noEmit --project cf-proxy/tsconfig.json`
- `bun run format:check`
- `git diff --check`

title: Improve TSX transform recovery and errors
description: Fixes TSX transform errors by allowing block comments between component props and providing clearer error messages for syntax issues.
body:
## What changed

- Retry TSX transforms by converting a single JSX-style block comment between component props into the equivalent valid block-comment syntax.
- Only accept a repair when that isolated change makes the entire file parse, preserving valid JSX child comments and comment-like string contents.
- Separate transform failures from runtime evaluation failures.
- Format remaining syntax errors with the source filename, line/column, surrounding source, and a caret.
- Rename the opaque `Eval compiled js error` runtime wrapper to `Error evaluating "<file>"`.

## Root cause

The reported circuit placed `{/* ... */}` between `<chip` props. That form is valid for JSX children but invalid inside an opening tag. Sucrase reported it as an unexpected token, and `importLocalFile` then caught the transform exception together with runtime exceptions and mislabeled it as a compiled-JS evaluation failure.

## User impact

The reported generated TSX now evaluates without requiring the user to rewrite unrelated constructs such as the anonymous default export, `Array.from` arrow callback, em-dash, or `$` identifier. Other syntax failures now point directly at source instead of presenting an opaque nested transform/eval message.

## Validation

- `bun test tests/repros/jsx-comment-between-props.test.tsx tests/examples/example09-not-defined-component.test.tsx`
- `bunx tsc --noEmit`
- `bun run format:check`
- `bun run build`
- Full suite exercised 147 passing tests before worker tests hit the checkout's initially missing build artifacts; after building, all affected worker/error tests passed on focused rerun.

title: Fix physical net in hypergraph pathing
description: Fixes routing failures caused by incorrect physical net IDs in hypergraph construction, ensuring consistent net identity during autorouting.
body:
It’s basically two PRs:

* Merge this PR into the current branch: https://github.com/tscircuit/tscircuit-autorouter/pull/1733
* Put the rest of the changes in this PR.


## Summary

- require connMap-backed canonical physical-net IDs when building hypergraphs
- carry canonical IDs through tiny-hypergraph/BFS routing and fail loudly when they are missing
- canonicalize and deduplicate region connectivity aliases without mutating inputs
- add SRJ23 sample 003 and focused buildHyperGraph regression coverage
- refresh only routing snapshots changed by the corrected net identity

## Root cause

Aliases belonging to the same physical net could receive different routing identities because hypergraph construction used connection/root names instead of the connectivity map. Selective reripping then treated same-net copper as a blocker and repeatedly failed to route SRJ23 sample 003.

## Validation

Before publication:
- production build and declaration generation passed
- TypeScript checking passed
- full test run completed with only expected snapshot changes
- all affected snapshot tests passed after targeted updates
- two independent code reviews found no correctness or scope issues

title: Preserve SRJ root connection identities in hypergraph pathing
description: Preserves SRJ root connection identities for accurate trace-width and connectivity lookups in hypergraph pathing.
body:
## Summary

- derive `PortPoint.rootConnectionName` only from `simpleRouteConnection.__rootConnectionNames`
- keep `mutuallyConnectedNetworkId` limited to canonical physical-net grouping and BFS blocking
- preserve SRJ root identity across tiny-hypergraph serialization using the original connection metadata

## Root cause

The canonical connectivity-map net ID was reused as `rootConnectionName`. Downstream solvers interpret `rootConnectionName` as an original SRJ/configuration identity, so trace-width lookup could receive a generated connectivity ID instead of the SRJ connection name.

## Impact

Routes retain their original SRJ root identity for downstream trace-width and connectivity lookups while canonical physical-net behavior remains unchanged.

## Validation

- `bun run build`
- `bun test tests/tinyhypergraph-terminal-port-ids.test.ts tests/features/build-hypergraph-canonical-net.test.ts --timeout 9999999`
- `bun test tests/pipeline-immutability/autorouting-pipeline3-hg-port-point-pathing.test.ts tests/pipeline-immutability/autorouting-pipeline4-tiny-hypergraph.test.ts --timeout 9999999`

Stacked on #1728.

title: Fix alpha handling for opaque glTF materials
description: Fixes rendering of opaque glTF materials by ensuring OPAQUE fragments write a fully opaque alpha value, while MASK fragments become opaque after passing the cutoff, and BLEND materials retain their fractional alpha behavior.
body:
## Summary

- force glTF `OPAQUE` fragments to write a fully opaque alpha value
- make `MASK` fragments fully opaque after they pass the cutoff
- preserve the existing fractional-alpha behavior for `BLEND` materials
- add focused value-level regression coverage for all three alpha modes
- add a visual PNG snapshot showing opaque, masked, and blended materials side by side
- document in `AGENTS.md` that tests need visual snapshots

## Root cause

The software renderer used `baseColorFactor[3]` as the output PNG alpha even when a material's glTF `alphaMode` was `OPAQUE`. This allowed a material such as `baseColorFactor: [0.5, 0.5, 0.5, 0.5]` with `alphaMode: "OPAQUE"` to produce half-transparent PNG pixels. It caused opaque enclosure geometry generated by circuit-json-to-gltf to appear as checkerboard transparency in core snapshots.

## Impact

Opaque enclosure and component geometry now renders with alpha 255, matching glTF alpha-mode semantics. Masked materials remain discarded below the cutoff and are opaque above it; blended materials remain translucent.

## Validation

- `bun test` — 26 passing, including the new visual snapshot
- `bun run format:check`
- `bunx tsc --noEmit`
- `bun run build`
- rendered the core enclosure GLB end to end with a white background; output alpha range is 255–255

title: add DRC-scored via-in-pad topology repairs
description: Add opt-in via-in-pad topology candidates to GlobalDrcForceImproveSolver, allowing GlobalDrcBranchPortfolioSolver to run these candidates as its final internal phase, relocating terminal-side vias and moving fully single-layer terminal routes using pad-centered transitions, while validating that the drilled hole fits inside each connected single-layer terminal pad before proposing a candidate and scoring every valid topology candidate with the caller-provided full DRC evaluator.
body:
## Summary

- add opt-in via-in-pad topology candidates to `GlobalDrcForceImproveSolver`
- let `GlobalDrcBranchPortfolioSolver` run those candidates as its final internal phase
- relocate terminal-side vias and move fully single-layer terminal routes using pad-centered transitions
- accept the board's via drill diameter independently from the outer via land diameter
- validate that the drilled hole fits inside each connected single-layer terminal pad before proposing a candidate
- score every valid topology candidate with the caller-provided full DRC evaluator and reject regressions

## Why

Dense terminal fields can require a layer transition inside the connected terminal pad. Local point-force moves cannot make that topology change, so the repair belongs in the global DRC solver that already compares alternative route candidates.

Via-in-pad eligibility is a solver invariant, not an autorouter-specific mask. The important containment rule is that the drilled hole remains inside the pad copper. A same-net outer via land may extend past the terminal pad, so comparing the full via diameter to the pad dimensions incorrectly rejects valid repairs on small pads.

`viaHoleDiameter` is therefore forwarded through `GlobalDrcForceImproveSolver` to both via-in-pad mutations. When it is omitted, the existing route via diameter remains the conservative fallback for callers that do not distinguish land and drill sizes.

## Dataset 18 verification

With the corresponding autorouter integration pinned to this commit, the full 16-sample Blacksmith benchmark falls from the regressed artifact's 548 total DRCs to 421. The affected samples are:

| Sample | Regressed artifact | Fixed Blacksmith run |
|---:|---:|---:|
| 13 | 105 | 54 |
| 14 | 138 | 93 |
| 16 | 5 | 5 |

The test suite also covers a 0.15 mm drill accepted in a 0.20 mm terminal pad with a 0.30 mm outer via land, while rejecting a 0.10 mm pad.

## Validation

- `bun test --timeout 9999999` — 29 pass, 0 fail
- `bun run typecheck` — pass
- `bun run formatcheck` — pass
- consuming autorouter `bun run build` — pass, including declaration generation
- full Dataset 18 Blacksmith benchmark — 421 total DRCs, all 16 samples completed

title: Use shared footprint comparison from circuit-json-to-footprinter
description: Removes duplicated footprint geometry and comparison logic from fast-footprint-compare and reuses the shared implementation from circuit-json-to-footprinter, ensuring consistent footprint comparison and reducing frontend bundle size.
body:
## Summary

This PR removes duplicated footprint geometry and comparison logic from `fast-footprint-compare` and reuses the shared implementation from `circuit-json-to-footprinter`.

## Changes

- Removed duplicated footprint geometry
- Removed duplicated comparison implementation
- Removed duplicated preview types
- Switched to shared APIs:
  - `footprinterStringToPreview`
  - `circuitJsonToPreview`
  - `summarizeCopperComparison`
- Reused shared preview and comparison types
- Kept API validation and UI logic inside the application

## Why?

Previously both repositories implemented their own footprint comparison logic, which caused the implementations to diverge.

After this change:

- `circuit-json-to-footprinter` owns footprint geometry and comparison
- `fast-footprint-compare` owns fetching, validation, APIs, and UI

This ensures discovery, comparison, and visualization all use the same implementation.

## Bundle improvements

Using the lightweight comparison entry significantly reduces the frontend bundle size by avoiding the discovery engine in the browser.

## Validation

- bun test
- bun run build
- bun run typecheck

## Notes

This PR depends on the corresponding `circuit-json-to-footprinter` release. Update the package version before merging.

title: Prioritize pill-shaped footprint seeds
description: Prioritizes one exact pad-shape seed per footprint family when the target contains pill pads, while keeping ordinary rectangular targets on the existing seed-selection path, and updates the footprinter for DFN pill-pad generation.
body:
## What changed

- prioritize one exact pad-shape seed per footprint family when the target contains pill pads
- keep ordinary rectangular targets on the existing seed-selection path
- update `@tscircuit/footprinter` to 0.0.382 for DFN pill-pad generation
- add a real C92489 BME280 regression
- carry the orientation-aware seed initialization from #22 onto `main` because #22 was merged into the former #20 feature branch rather than the default branch

## Why

The matcher generated `_pillpads` variants, but its ten-seed optimization cap was usually filled by several rotations of rectangular candidates before the shape-compatible DFN seed was optimized. Selecting the strongest pill-compatible candidate from distinct families gives the optimizer access to exact geometry without increasing the optimization budget.

The heuristic is limited to pill-bearing targets. Applying it to normal rectangular pads can promote a lower-IoU package-family candidate through the domain-score bonus.

## Benchmark

Sequential run across all 250 jlc5000 boards:

- current `main`: 97/250 at 98%+ copper IoU (38.8%)
- orientation-only baseline: 133/250 (53.2%)
- this branch: 140/250 (56.0%)
- seven new exact matches over the orientation baseline
- eight raw-IoU improvements and zero regressions
- zero benchmark errors

New exact matches include BME280, SN74LVC2G132DCUR, U3018, AMC1301DWVR, PC400J00000F, SN74LVC1G123DCTR, and TC7WH157FU.

## Checks

- `bun test` — 26 passing
- `bun run typecheck`
- `bun run format:check`
- `bun run build`
- full sequential jlc5000 benchmark

title: Initialize rotated seeds with oriented dimensions
description: Swaps width and height heuristics for seeds rotated by 90 or 270, using oriented dimensions for parameter detection and optimization, and adds regression tests for accurate recovery of rotated footprints.
body:
## What

- swap width and height heuristics when optimizing seeds rotated by 90° or 270°
- use those oriented dimensions for active-parameter detection, initialization, step sizing, and bounds
- add a regression for an exact rotated SOIC/pill-pad recovery

## Why

Target bounds are measured in world axes, while footprinter width and height parameters remain in the seed local axes. Starting a quarter-turned seed with the unswapped dimensions sends the optimizer toward the wrong geometry and can leave otherwise exact matches below the benchmark threshold.

## Benchmark

Sequential run across all 250 jlc5000 boards:

- before: 97/250 at 98%+ copper IoU (38.8%)
- after: 133/250 at 98%+ copper IoU (53.2%)
- 36 new passing components
- zero threshold regressions
- zero benchmark errors

Examples reaching exact IoU include L78L05ACD13TR, TLP291-4, and 74HC14D.

## Checks

- bun test (24 passing)
- bun run typecheck
- bun run format:check
- bun run build
- full sequential jlc5000 benchmark

Stacked on #20, which supplies quad pill-pad support and @tscircuit/footprinter 0.0.381.

body:
## Summary

This PR centralizes footprint preview generation and comparison logic into `circuit-json-to-footprinter`, making it the single source of truth for footprint geometry, copper comparison, and hole comparison.

## Changes

- Added shared footprint preview generation
- Added shared hole IoU comparison
- Added shared footprint bounds and rasterization
- Added shared comparison APIs:
  - `circuitJsonToPreview`
  - `footprinterStringToPreview`
  - `compareFootprints`
  - `summarizeCopperComparison`
  - `getFootprintBounds`
- Added lightweight `/compare` entry point for browser usage
- Added hole-aware discovery scoring
- Added support for:
  - rectangular plated holes
  - hole offsets
  - rotated slots
  - independent pad/hole rotation

## Why?

Previously both `circuit-json-to-footprinter` and `fast-footprint-compare` maintained separate geometry implementations. They had already started to diverge, causing different comparison behavior.

After this PR, all footprint geometry lives in one place.

## API changes

- `PreviewPad` now supports holes
- Discovery candidates include `holeIntersectionOverUnion`
- New comparison APIs exported
- Existing `circuitJsonToFootprinter` API remains unchanged

## Validation

- bun test
- bun run build
- bun run typecheck
- npm pack --dry-run

## Notes

This PR should be released before updating `fast-footprint-compare`.

title: Fix Node-compatible package exports
description: Changes the package exports to publish compiled JavaScript and declarations from dist instead of raw TypeScript from lib, enabling plain Node consumers to import the package without a TypeScript runtime loader.
body:
## Summary

- publish compiled JavaScript and declarations from `dist` instead of raw TypeScript from `lib`
- add an explicit package `exports` map and run the build automatically during `npm pack`/publish
- add a Node 22 package self-import check to CI

## Root cause

`@tscircuit/create-fdm-enclosure@0.0.2` publishes `lib/index.ts` as its `main` and `module` entrypoint. Bun can load that source directly, but the Node runtime used by `svg.tscircuit.com` fails with `ERR_UNKNOWN_FILE_EXTENSION`, causing render requests to return a generic HTTP 500 before the handler can run.

## Impact

The next published version will contain `dist/index.js`, `dist/index.d.ts`, and the source map. Plain Node consumers, including Next.js/Vercel server routes, can import the package without a TypeScript runtime loader.

## Validation

body:
## Summary

- bootstrap `@tscircuit/create-fdm-enclosure` as a typed solver-pattern library
- expose a small millimetre-based input for board dimensions, optional enclosure overrides, and rect/pill/circle wall apertures
- implement a four-stage `BasePipelineSolver`: resolve dimensions, create the open-top shell, create aperture cutouts, and compose the final JSCAD plan
- keep shape, margin, wall orientation, and placement logic in `lib/apertures/create-aperture-cutout-plan.ts`
- add validation for board fit, floor intersections, wall bounds, and explicit dimensions
- add Cosmos fixtures for a USB-C pill aperture, multiple apertures on all four walls, and explicit dimensions
- combine the standard solver debugger's step/animate controls with an interactive, downloadable GLB preview
- add SVG solver snapshots and white-background, grid-free GLB render snapshots

## Input model

All values are millimetres. The enclosure is centred on X/Y with its outside floor at Z = 0. Apertures select a wall and provide a signed offset along that wall plus `centerZ` above the floor. Width, height, and depth are inferred from the board and configured clearances when omitted.

## Scope

This PR owns enclosure planning and cutout generation only. The follow-up core integration will translate `<enclosure.fdm.Box />` and nested `<enclosure.cutoutaperture />` metadata into this solver input and emit the returned JSCAD plan.

## Visual snapshots

- [USB-C pill enclosure](https://github.com/tscircuit/create-fdm-enclosure/blob/agent/initial-enclosure-solver/tests/__snapshots__/inferred-usb-c-pill.snap.png)

## Validation

- `bun run build:site`

title: add configurable LGA footprints
description: Adds a parameterized LGA footprint family with configurable grid dimensions, pitch, copper envelope, pad dimensions, and optional pill pads, supporting unequal pad counts and two-sided LGA packages.
body:
## What changed

- add a parameterized `lga` footprint family with configurable `gridAxB`, pitch, copper envelope, pad dimensions, and optional pill pads
- support unequal pad counts on opposing sides and two-sided LGA packages through zero-width grid dimensions such as `grid3x0`
- add pin-one silkscreen and courtyard geometry
- add SVG snapshot coverage for rectangular, pill-pad, and two-sided LGA variants

## Why

Several popular JLCPCB parts use perimeter LGA land patterns that cannot be represented accurately by QFN/DFN generators. The side-oriented grid allows the matcher to express those exact pad layouts without adding a one-off footprint for each package.

In a local `jlc5000` benchmark at 95% copper IoU, pairing this generator with matcher inference raises coverage from 171/250 (68.4%) to 179/250 (71.6%). Eight additional parts cross the threshold, including LIS3DHTR, LSM6DS3TR-C, SC7A20HTR, ICP-20100, MSM261DGT003, and SC7A20TR.

## Validation

- `bun test` — 462 pass, 0 fail
- `bun run build`
- `git diff --check`
- visually inspected the generated SVG snapshots
- full sequential 250-board JLCPCB benchmark — 179/250 at 95% IoU, 0 errors

title: Add thermal pads to dual-row footprints
description: Add explicit thermal pad support to SOIC, DFN, TSSOP, SSOP, MSOP, and VSSOP footprints, enabling the representation of exposed-pad dual-row packages with a centered rectangular SMT pad and enhancing the typed builder API with this parameter.
body:
## Summary

- add explicit `thermalpadWxH` support to SOIC, DFN, TSSOP, SSOP, MSOP, and VSSOP footprints
- emit a centered rectangular SMT pad with the `thermalpad` port hint
- expose the parameter through the typed builder API
- add an SVG snapshot and geometry assertion for every supported family

## Why

Exposed-pad dual-row packages currently cannot be represented by these generators, forcing `circuit-json-to-footprinter` toward QFN/MLP candidates even when the perimeter is SOIC-, TSSOP-, or MSOP-like. A forced-family audit of JLC5000 found 13 current boards that can reach at least 98% copper IoU once the matcher can select these forms.

This is the generator prerequisite. A local end-to-end experiment also showed the matcher must compute topology from perimeter pads after excluding the center thermal pad; otherwise these targets are classified as four-sided and the new seeds are not selected. That matcher follow-up should land after this API is published.

## Validation

- `bun test` — 458 passed
- `bun test tests/dual-row-thermalpad.test.ts` — 6 passed
- `bun run build`
- visually inspected all six SVG snapshots
- `git diff --check`

title: fix: distinguish EasyEDA HTTP 403 rate limiting from component lookup failure
description: Fixes the error message for EasyEDA API rate limiting to clearly indicate a 403 status instead of a generic lookup failure, providing users with specific guidance on the rate limit and cooldown period.
body:
Fixes #409 — the error-text distinction (suggestion 3 of the issue).

## Problem

EasyEDA's component API rate-limits by IP and answers **HTTP 403** once tripped (not 429, no `Retry-After`). `fetchEasyEDAComponent` treated any non-success response as a lookup failure, so a throttled batch surfaced as:

```
Error: Failed to search for the component
```

which points the user at the part number. The part is fine; the request was throttled — as measured in #409, every LCSC id 403s identically mid-cooldown and recovers after ~2 minutes.

## Change (minimal, one concern)

- A **403** on either the search request or the component-details request now throws a rate-limit-specific error naming the part, stating it's an IP-based limit, and giving the ~2 minute cooldown hint from the issue's measurements.
- Other non-success statuses keep their existing messages but now include the HTTP status code.
- `Component not found` (successful response, empty result list) is **unchanged** — it stays distinct from rate limiting, which is the core complaint in #409.

Retry/backoff, serialising the parts-engine batch, and CDN proxying (suggestions 1, 2, 4) are deliberately left as follow-ups — they're behaviour/architecture decisions, and per review feedback on other PRs I'm keeping one change per PR.

## Tests (red→green)

`tests/fetch-tests/rate-limit-403.test.ts` — uses the existing injectable `fetch` option, so **no network** (unlike the live-API fetch tests, these can't themselves be rate-limited):

1. search-request 403 → `/rate limit/i` error (was: generic lookup failure) — **red before, green after**
2. component-details 403 → `/rate limit/i` error — **red before, green after**
3. empty search result → still exactly `Component not found` (guards the distinction)
4. non-403 failure (500) → message includes `HTTP 500` — **red before, green after**

`bun test` on the new file: 4 pass. `tsc --noEmit` clean. `biome check` clean.

Note: #393 (May) adds part numbers to these messages but doesn't distinguish status codes; this PR is orthogonal and touches only the status-code branches.

title: fix: propagate manufacturerPartNumber in resonator and 10 other components

## Summary

Propagate `manufacturerPartNumber` (and `mfn` alias) to `source_component.manufacturer_part_number` in every component whose `doInitialSourceRender()` override was silently dropping it.

Each component already wrote `supplier_part_numbers` and `display_name`; `manufacturer_part_number` was simply forgotten — a copy-paste omission dating back to when the base class first added the field in PR #552.

Resonator, OpAmp, PowerSource, Ammeter, Inductor, CurrentSource, PushButton, VoltageSource, TestPoint, PinHeader, Battery

## Implementation

Each fix adds a single line to the `source_component.insert({...})` call:

```ts
manufacturer_part_number: props.manufacturerPartNumber ?? props.mfn,
```

Matching the pattern already used by the base class (`NormalComponent.ts:692`) and every properly-implemented peer (Resistor, Capacitor, Diode, Mosfet, Switch, Chip, Jumper, SolderJumper, Pinout, Connector, Led, etc.).

## Testing

title: repro: mosfet doesn't propagate manufacturerPartNumber and supplierPartNumbers
description: Fixes the issue where manufacturerPartNumber and supplierPartNumbers are not propagated to the generated source_component for the mosfet  component.
body:
`manufacturerPartNumber` and `supplierPartNumbers` passed to `<mosfet />` are
silently dropped — they never appear in the generated `source_component`.

The test asserts that
`source_component.manufacturer_part_number` and
`source_component.supplier_part_numbers` **should** contain the provided
values, but they are currently `undefined`.

Same pattern as the `<switch />` fix in PR #2765:
`Mosfet#doInitialSourceRender()` overrides the base class without calling
`super.doInitialSourceRender()` and omits these fields from its
`source_component.insert()` call.

## Why

[schematic-trace-solver#692](https://github.com/tscircuit/schematic-trace-solver/pull/692) fixes a cleanup bug where traces on the same global net were treated as obstacles. That could force a pin trace to turn vertically before traveling directly toward a nearby VCC connection.

body:
## Summary

- renders the canonical 16 USB-C source and schematic ports when standard connector part resolution fails
- keeps the existing Parts Engine failure warning and does not synthesize PCB pads or other physical footprint geometry
- shares one canonical USB-C pin definition between fetched-part normalization and the fallback
- updates the merged repro to snapshot and assert the corrected fallback behavior

## Root cause

`<connector standard="usb_c" />` previously derived all ports from fetched part Circuit JSON. A failed `findPart` call, failed `fetchPartCircuitJson` call, empty Circuit JSON result, or unavailable fetch method therefore left the connector with no ports and rendered an empty schematic box.

The failure handler now adds canonical logical ports only after resolution has failed. Successful resolution continues to use the fetched part's manufacturer-specific pin numbering and physical footprint.

## Validation

- USB-C Parts Engine failure paths: fetch rejection, part not found, Circuit JSON not found, and missing fetch method
- successful USB-C fetch, aliases, MPN fallback, CAD model priority, canonical pin ordering across two physical parts, and explicit-footprint bypass
- `bunx tsc --noEmit`
- `bun run build`
- full `bun test`: 1,214 passed and 31 skipped; one unrelated differential-pair PCB snapshot mismatch also reproduces on a clean `origin/main` worktree

title: Infer schematic sheets from schematic box connections
description: Adds support for assigning a board-level schematicbox  to a schematicsheet  with schSheetName, and automatically places components connected to the boxs represented chip pins on the same sheet.
body:
## What changed

This PR adds support for assigning a board-level `<schematicbox />` to a `<schematicsheet />` with `schSheetName`, and automatically places components connected to the box's represented chip pins on the same sheet.

- updates `@tscircuit/props` to `^0.0.589` for `schematicbox.schSheetName`
- adds sheet resolution for sibling `<schematicbox />` elements
- infers a connected component's sheet from source connectivity when exactly one schematic sheet is implied
- preserves explicit or inherited `schSheetName` placement
- leaves components unassigned when their connections imply multiple sheets
- adds stacked schematic and PCB snapshots covering two boxes that represent one physical chip

This follows the prop addition in [tscircuit/props#747](https://github.com/tscircuit/props/pull/747).

## Example

```tsx
<schematicsheet name="Power Sheet" sheetIndex={0} />

<schematicbox
  name="U1 Power"
  schSheetName="Power Sheet"
  chipRef=".U1"
  pinLabels={{ pin1: "VDD", pin2: "GND", pin3: "RESET" }}
  schPinArrangement={{
    leftSide: ["pin1", "pin2", "pin3"],
    rightSide: [],
  }}
/>

<resistor
  name="R1"
  resistance="10k"
  connections={{ pin1: "U1.RESET" }}
/>
```

`R1` does not specify `schSheetName`. Its connection to `U1.RESET`, represented by the `U1 Power` schematic box, places it on `Power Sheet` automatically.

## Implementation

Source connectivity keys are available before schematic rendering. For each schematic box, the implementation maps its represented chip pins to the box's resolved sheet. A normal component receives an inferred sheet only when all matching connections resolve to one unique sheet.

The physical chip referenced by the schematic boxes is excluded from inference so that its regular schematic symbol is not rendered alongside the box representation.

## Visual snapshots

### Stacked schematic

![Two schematic sheets with connected components inferred from schematic-box pins](https://raw.githubusercontent.com/tscircuit/core/da17db77c9646a0300056c46c969189ed7c5a751/tests/features/schematic-sheet/__snapshots__/schematic-sheet05-schematic_stacked.snap.svg)

### PCB

![One physical U1 connected to both resistors on the PCB](https://raw.githubusercontent.com/tscircuit/core/da17db77c9646a0300056c46c969189ed7c5a751/tests/features/schematic-sheet/__snapshots__/schematic-sheet05-pcb.snap.svg)

## Validation

- `bun test tests/features/schematic-sheet` — 5 passed
- related multi-sheet regression tests — 4 passed
- `./node_modules/.bin/tsc --noEmit`
- Biome formatting

title: fix: defaultTraceWidth/nominalTraceWidth board props now control autorouted trace width
description: Fixes autorouting to respect defaultTraceWidth and nominalTraceWidth properties for trace width control.
body:
Fixes #2620

## What & why

`<board defaultTraceWidth="0.3mm">` (and `nominalTraceWidth`) had no effect on autorouted trace width — every autorouted segment came out at 0.15mm, exactly as diagnosed in the issue:

1. `Group._runLocalAutorouting` only read `props.minTraceWidth` / `props.nominalTraceWidth`; `defaultTraceWidth` was accepted by the props schema but never read anywhere in core.
2. `getSimpleRouteJsonFromCircuitJson` filled each connection's `nominalTraceWidth`/`width` from `minTraceWidth ?? board.min_trace_width ?? 0.1` and only stored its `nominalTraceWidth` parameter as the top-level `SimpleRouteJson.nominalTraceWidth`, which the capacity autorouter's `TraceWidthSolver` ignores (it consumes per-connection `nominalTraceWidth`).

## Changes

- **`Group._runLocalAutorouting`**: the nominal width passed to the SRJ builder is now `props.defaultTraceWidth ?? props.nominalTraceWidth ?? minTraceWidth`.
- **`getSimpleRouteJsonFromCircuitJson`**: connections without an explicit width (e.g. from a `<trace thickness>`) now default to the `nominalTraceWidth` parameter when provided; `minTraceWidth` remains the routing lower bound only (`SimpleRouteJson.minTraceWidth` is still computed from it).
- **Legacy remote solve-endpoint path**: passes the same `defaultTraceWidth ?? nominalTraceWidth` preference (previously passed raw `this.props.nominalTraceWidth` and also dropped `defaultTraceWidth`).
- Behavior is unchanged when neither prop is set (still 0.15mm), and an explicit `<trace thickness>` still takes precedence.

## Tests

- Updated `tests/components/primitive-components/trace-default-trace-width.test.tsx`, which previously asserted the buggy behavior (`width === 0.15` with a "Bug repro" note saying the fixed behavior should be 0.3) — it now asserts 0.3mm, and its PCB snapshot was regenerated (the routed trace is visibly wider).
- Added `tests/features/board-default-trace-width-autorouting.test.tsx` covering: `nominalTraceWidth` on the board, explicit `thickness` precedence over `defaultTraceWidth`, and the unchanged 0.15mm default.

## How I tested

Ran locally with bun 1.3.14 (full suite is too heavy for my machine, so I ran the directories surrounding the change):

- `bun test tests/features/board-default-trace-width-autorouting.test.tsx tests/components/primitive-components/trace-default-trace-width.test.tsx tests/utils/autorouting/` — all pass (the new tests fail before the fix, reproducing the issue exactly: 0.15 instead of 0.3/0.25)
- `bun test tests/utils/ tests/components/primitive-components/ tests/components/normal-components/ tests/features/ tests/subcircuits/ tests/groups/ tests/drc/ tests/breakout/` — no new failures (`auto-jumper-autorouting`, `schematic-section-rp2040`, and `differential-pair/routing-phase` time out identically on unmodified `main` on my machine)
- `bunx tsc --noEmit` — clean
- `bun run format` — no changes

title: fix: <switch /> drops supplierPartNumbers/manufacturerPartNumber, part missing from BOM
description: Fixes issue where switch  component fails to propagate supplierPartNumbers and manufacturerPartNumber to the BOM, causing parts to be omitted without warning.
body:
Fixes tscircuit/tscircuit#4056

## Problem

`<switch />` accepts `supplierPartNumbers` (valid per `SwitchProps extends CommonComponentProps`), but `Switch.doInitialSourceRender()` builds the `source_component` with a fixed field list that omits `supplier_part_numbers` and `manufacturer_part_number`. The value silently disappears and the part never reaches the BOM — no type error, no warning.

Every other component type (`<chip />`, `<pushbutton />`, `<led />`, `<resistor />`, …) passes these through, e.g. PushButton:

```ts
supplier_part_numbers: props.supplierPartNumbers,
```

## Fix

Pass both fields through in `Switch.doInitialSourceRender()`, matching the pattern used by the other normal components (`manufacturerPartNumber ?? mfn` alias handling as in Resistor/Capacitor):

```ts
manufacturer_part_number: props.manufacturerPartNumber ?? props.mfn,
supplier_part_numbers: props.supplierPartNumbers,
```

## Test

Red→green test `tests/components/normal-components/switch-supplier-part-numbers.test.tsx`: renders a `<switch />` with `supplierPartNumbers={{ jlcpcb: ["C2681570"] }}` and `manufacturerPartNumber`, asserts both land on the `source_component`. Fails on main (`supplier_part_numbers` empty), passes with this change. Existing switch tests still pass; `tsc --noEmit` clean.

title: Ban string-keyed maps in code guidelines
description: Add a shared code guideline that bans Mapstring, ... and requires named or branded key types such as MapSchematicPortId, ... to enhance code clarity and prevent misuse.
body:
## Summary

- add a shared code guideline that bans `Map<string, ...>`
- require named or branded key types such as `Map<SchematicPortId, ...>`
- append the rule as guideline 8 without renumbering existing sections

## Why

Named key types expose the domain meaning of map keys and make code easier to understand and harder to misuse.

`@tscircuit/plop` downloads `CODE_GUIDELINES.md` directly from `tscircuit/handbook/guides/code.md`, so this source document must be updated for the rule to appear in the plop-provided guidelines.

## Impact

Once merged, new copies of `CODE_GUIDELINES.md` downloaded through `@tscircuit/plop` will include the rule automatically.

## Validation

- `git diff --check main...HEAD`
- documentation-only change; no runtime tests required

title: Render multi-sheet schematics as stacked SVGs
description: Changes the rendering of multi-sheet schematics to output as stacked SVGs, while retaining single-sheet behavior for circuits with zero or one sheet.
body:
## Summary

- render schematic output with `convertCircuitJsonToStackedSchematicSheetsSvg`
- retain existing single-sheet behavior through the converter's built-in fallback
- add a docs-shaped regression case with one chip represented on two schematic sheets
- snapshot both sheets, their section labels, ports, and connections in one SVG

## Root cause

`svg.tscircuit.com` always called `convertCircuitJsonToSchematicSvg`, which intentionally selects only one schematic sheet. Multi-sheet Circuit JSON therefore produced only the default sheet even though `circuit-to-svg` already provides a stacked renderer.

The regression also keeps the source chip before the `schematicbox` references, matching the declaration order required by the currently locked core runtime.

## Impact

Docs previews and other schematic SVG consumers now receive every schematic sheet stacked vertically. Circuits with zero or one sheet continue through the existing single-sheet renderer because the stacked converter falls back automatically.

## Validation

- `bun run format:check`
- `bun test tests/multi-sheet-schematic-svg.test.ts tests/schematic-svg.test.ts tests/no-board-wrapper-schematic.test.ts`
- `bun run build`

title: Document the schematicbox element
description: Adds documentation for the schematicbox  element, detailing its usage, sizing modes, and properties.
body:
## Summary

- add the missing `<schematicbox />` element reference
- document both non-`chipRef` sizing modes from the core examples
- explain how fixed-size boxes use `width`, `height`, `schX`, and `schY`
- explain how overlay boxes calculate bounds from selected ports, including ports from multiple components
- document padding overrides, title placement, and solid/dashed outlines
- explain how `chipRef`, local `pinLabels`, and `schPinArrangement` represent part of a chip
- list the supported schematic box props

## Behavior without `chipRef`

Without `chipRef`, `<schematicbox />` is a visual schematic primitive and does not create a component or ports. It must use exactly one sizing strategy:

1. `width` and `height` create a box centered at `schX` and `schY`.
2. A non-empty `overlay` array resolves existing port selectors after layout and calculates a padded bounding box.

The examples are based on `example24-schematic-box-component.test.tsx` and `example26-schematic-box.title.test.tsx` in `tscircuit/core`.

## Validation

- `bun run typecheck`
- `bun run build`
- `bun test tests/examples/example24-schematic-box-component.test.tsx tests/examples/example26-schematic-box.title.test.tsx` in `tscircuit/core`

title: Pin bounded exact DRC repair for SRJ18
description: Pins high-density-repair03 to an exact commit, refreshes route snapshots, and updates DRC fixture to eliminate trace errors, improving DRC error resolution for SRJ18 samples.
body:
## What changed

- pin `high-density-repair03` to the exact commit from tscircuit/high-density-repair03#30
- refresh only the route snapshots changed by the exact DRC repair
- update the `bugreport-b5b3b9d8` DRC fixture from two trace errors to zero
- bound exact trace-detour variant search when a DRC error does not resolve to a trace pair

## Why

SRJ18 sample 10 reached the repair stage with three exact DRC errors that the existing repair portfolio could not remove. The paired repair PR adds same-net via clearance repair, targeted trace-pair moves/detours, and bounded search for large boards.

The first benchmark-all run exposed a runtime regression: unresolved exact trace-pair errors could cycle through detour variants indefinitely because inapplicable variants did not consume the candidate budget. That loop is now limited to one pass over the finite variant set.

## Impact

## Summary

- prevent schematic traces from crossing the raw bounds of component text
- keep endpoint label padding relaxations without allowing traces through the text itself
- add local internal-segment detours for long-distance routes blocked by endpoint text
- preserve the existing `U1.14-RTEMP.1` upper trace instead of replacing it with fallback net labels
- add semantic regression assertions and update the affected snapshots

## Context

The TPS61222 repro from tscircuit/core#2785 routed GND through the manufacturer part number. Treating raw text bounds as hard obstacles fixed that collision, but initially caused an existing long-distance trace to disappear because shifting its entire rail overlapped another trace.

Long-distance routes now detour locally around endpoint text while preserving their endpoint legs. This keeps the upper trace present and avoids both the MPN and reference-designator text.

title: Untangle merged-label trace crossings
description: Detects strict internal perpendicular crossings in merged-label trace bundles that connect the same component pair and generates clearance-aware detour candidates inside the existing untangling solver.
body:
## Summary

- detect strict internal perpendicular crossings in merged-label trace bundles that connect the same component pair
- generate clearance-aware detour candidates inside the existing untangling solver
- continue rip-up/reroute from a moved trace when resolving one crossing transfers it to a neighboring trace
- add a direct repro47 intersection assertion and update three visually verified solver snapshots

## Root cause

`UntangleTraceSubsolver` only processed an L-shaped turn when both arms intersected trace obstacles. A crossing isolated to one internal arm was therefore skipped, leaving avoidable braids such as repro47's middle overlap.

The new path is structural rather than fixture-specific: it uses merged-label bundle membership, component-pair identity, strict segment intersection geometry, chip clearance, and collision scoring. Ordinary schematic crossings outside these bundles remain untouched.

## Validation

- `bun test` — 136 passed, 4 skipped
- TypeScript typecheck passed
- formatting check passed
- `bun run build` passed
- linked `tscircuit/core` schematic snapshot suite — 288 passed, 5 skipped

title: Preserve same-net trace alignment during cleanup
description: Preserves same-net trace branches as visual alignment constraints during turn minimization, ensuring better alignment and fewer turns in routing.
body:
## Summary

- preserve same-net trace branches as visual alignment constraints during turn minimization
- compare strict and endpoint-sharing relaxed routes by turns and rendered same-net complexity
- add the full `core` example35 input as a solver-owned regression and snapshot
- add an explicit example19 rail-alignment assertion
- restore only the example13 and example29 snapshots whose rails regressed under the broad 0.0.105 relaxation

## Root cause

The 0.0.105 cleanup change removed every trace on the target global net from the obstacle set. That fixed the VCC pin 1 detour, but it also made equivalent-turn alternatives legal. Candidate ordering then shifted repeated capacitor-chain rails toward one endpoint, producing the more complex example35 schematic.

A strict-wins-ties follow-up fixed example35 but regressed example19 by selecting an off-axis rail despite an equivalent route aligned with existing same-net geometry.

The cleanup now compares a strict route against a route that relaxes only endpoint-sharing same-net branches. Fewer turns win first. Equivalent-turn routes are scored by the number of distinct visible same-net collinear runs, then visible length. This keeps VCC direct-left, restores example35's local alignment, and preserves example19's simpler shared rail.

## Validation

- `bun test`: 136 passed, 4 skipped
- `bunx tsc --noEmit`
- `bun run format:check`
- `bun run build`
- example19 pipeline rendering is byte-identical to 0.0.105
- extracted example35 pipeline rendering is byte-identical to 0.0.104
- the VCC direct-left regression test remains passing

title: Fix same-net VCC trace detours
description: Fixes routing issues where VCC traces on the same net were incorrectly treated as obstacles, leading to unnecessary detours in the trace path.
body:
## Summary

- add a focused VCC regression where U1 pin 1 should route directly left into the shared pin 6 branch
- stop treating traces on the same global net as obstacles during turn minimization
- keep different-net traces blocking and refresh affected snapshots to capture the simpler same-net geometry

## Root cause

Turn minimization converted every other trace into a geometric obstacle, including traces on the target trace's own net. The direct pin 1 route therefore appeared blocked by the VCC branch and the solver retained the unnecessary upward detour. Same-net traces are valid junction targets, so only different-net traces should constrain this cleanup pass.

## Impact

VCC and other multi-branch nets can collapse unnecessary detours into clean coincident junctions while different nets remain isolated.

## Validation

- `bun test` — 135 passed, 4 skipped, 0 failed
- `bunx tsc --noEmit`
- `bun run format:check`
- `bun run build`
- `git diff --check`

title: Improve exact DRC repair for constrained routes
description: Restores the via-in-pad repair portfolio, repairs explicitly identified same-net via pairs, resolves exact trace pairs from canonical DRC identifiers, and implements targeted segment layer moves and bounded copper dogleg detours for low-error boards.
body:
## What changed

- restore the via-in-pad repair portfolio used by Pipeline 7
- repair explicitly identified same-net via pairs without enabling broad same-net repulsion in checker-backed runs
- resolve exact trace pairs from canonical DRC identifiers
- try targeted segment layer moves and bounded copper dogleg detours for low-error boards
- stop detour search after one pass through the finite variant set when an exact trace pair cannot be resolved
- skip the combinatorial via-in-pad phase on large boards that still have more than three canonical errors
- add focused regression coverage for every repair primitive, the large-board budget guard, and unresolved trace-pair termination

## Why

SRJ18 sample 10 ended with two constrained trace conflicts and one same-net via clearance error. The existing force solver primarily selected geometry by proximity to an error center, so it could move the wrong trace or ignore a same-net pair. Local movement could also stall when the only valid repair required a layer transition or a directed detour.

The first benchmark-all run also exposed an unbounded-loop case: an error with a trace identifier but no resolvable pair repeatedly tested inapplicable detour variants without consuming the candidate budget. The search now checks each variant at most once per solver step.

The new candidates use the exact trace/via ownership supplied by the canonical evaluator and are accepted only after full DRC evaluation.

## Impact

- SRJ18 relaxed-DRC clean samples improve from 2/16 to 3/16 (18.75%): samples 1, 5, and 10
- the existing SRJ18 clean samples remain clean
- DRC14 improves from 37/44 clean with 8 final errors to 41/44 clean with 3 final errors; no previously clean sample regresses
- all eight reproduced exact-stage timeout regressions from dataset01 and SRJ23 now finish in 0.1–8.0 seconds
- expensive topology candidates remain limited to low-error cases, while large high-error boards bypass the combinatorial phase

## Validation

- `bunx tsc --noEmit`
- `bun test` — 31 passed
- DRC14 — 41/44 clean, 73/76 DRC issues removed
- focused dataset01 timeout rerun — samples 49 and 57 complete with zero timeouts
- focused SRJ23 timeout rerun — samples 23, 37, 51, 73, 89, and 106 complete with zero timeouts
- focused SRJ18 samples 1, 5, and 10 with a 2000-second limit — 3/3 relaxed-DRC clean, zero timeouts

- TI wireless/MCU: [CC2340R5](https://www.ti.com/lit/ds/symlink/cc2340r5.pdf), [CC2745R10-Q1](https://www.ti.com/lit/ds/symlink/cc2745r10-q1.pdf), [CC3235SF](https://www.ti.com/lit/ds/symlink/cc3235sf.pdf), [MSPM0G3507](https://www.ti.com/lit/ds/symlink/mspm0g3507.pdf)

body: pin2 pad is missing due to unsupported polygon pads ## Summary This allows footprints such as SOT-89, which contain irregular copper pads, to be parsed and matched without approximating polygon pads as rectangles. - Add support for polygon-shaped `pcb_smtpad` elements. - Preserve polygon vertices and correctly handle absolute versus pad-relative coordinates. - Add polygon-aware bounds, rasterization, and footprint discovery. - Introduce discriminated preview geometry types to prevent invalid polygon states. - Validate malformed polygons with descriptive errors. - Centralize shared geometry calculations to avoid duplicated logic. ## Visual ![SOT-89 polygon SMT pad](https://github.com/tscircuit/circuit-json-to-footprinter/blob/if/tests/__snapshots__/polygon-smtpad-polygon-smtpad-sot89.snap.svg?raw=true) ## Testing - Added polygon SMT-pad parsing, bounds, rasterization, and discovery tests. - Added an SVG snapshot test for the SOT-89 polygon pad. - All 31 tests pass. - Type-checking, declaration builds, formatting, and diff checks pass.

title: match unequal-side LGA footprints
description: Adds support for unequal-side LGA footprints by discovering perimeter LGA footprints with unequal pad counts, inferring pad dimensions, and generating explicit LGA grid candidates, while updating the JLC audit threshold from 98 to 95 IoU.
body:
## What changed

- discover perimeter LGA footprints with unequal pad counts on horizontal and vertical sides
- infer pad length and width from each pad's side orientation
- generate explicit `lgaN_gridAxB` candidates, including two-sided grids such as `grid3x0`
- preserve pill-pad geometry and prioritize the inferred explicit LGA grid
- update the JLC audit threshold from 98% to 95% IoU
- require `@tscircuit/footprinter@^0.0.385`, which contains the LGA generator from tscircuit/footprinter#722

## Why

The matcher previously assumed that four-sided packages had the same number of pads on every side and that the longer pad dimension always pointed inward. That prevented it from expressing common JLCPCB LGA layouts such as 5x3, 4x3, 4x2, 3x2, 2x4, and two-sided LGA-6 packages.

The 95% audit threshold keeps coverage work focused on materially missing footprint families rather than negligible edge-shape differences.

## Impact

On the full sequential 250-board `jlc5000` benchmark, this moves eight additional parts above 95% copper IoU:

- coverage increases from 171/250 (68.4%) to 179/250 (71.6%)
- seven new matches reach 100% IoU
- FSUSB42UMX-TP reaches 98.11% IoU
- benchmark errors remain at zero

## Validation

- `bun test` — 31 pass, 0 fail
- `bun run typecheck`
- `bun run format:check`
- `bun run build`
- full sequential 250-board benchmark against published `@tscircuit/footprinter@0.0.385`

title: Match exposed-pad dual-row footprints
description: Classifies package topology from perimeter leads after removing an exposed center pad and generates thermal-pad seeds for various package families, improving benchmark performance significantly.
body:
## Summary

- classify package topology from perimeter leads after removing an exposed center pad
- generate thermal-pad seeds for DFN, MSOP, SOIC, SSOP, TSSOP, and VSSOP families
- generate both thermal-pad dimension orientations for 90-degree candidate rotations
- prioritize exposed-pad seeds during optimization
- prevent package-name hints from outranking candidates with exact pad shapes
- update to `@tscircuit/footprinter@^0.0.384`

## Root cause

The large center pad introduced a third coordinate cluster, so genuinely two-sided packages were classified as four-sided and only QFN/MLP/quad thermal candidates were generated. Non-square thermal pads were also kept in target orientation even when the candidate footprint rotated 90 degrees. Finally, a package-name hint could rank a rounded-rectangle SSOP candidate above a geometrically exact pill-pad DFN candidate.

## Impact

The JLC5000 benchmark improves from **140/250 (56.0%)** to **160/250 (64.0%)** components at or above 98% copper IoU: 20 additional passing components, an 8 percentage-point increase.

## Validation

- `bun test` — 28 passed
- `bun run typecheck`
- `bun run build`
- `bun run format:check`
- `git diff --check`
- `bun run benchmark --json /tmp/perimeter-thermal-benchmark-final.json --markdown /tmp/perimeter-thermal-benchmark-final.md` — 160/250, 64.0%, 0 errors
