title: Use obstacle-aware power trace expander
description: Add a whole-board autorouting phase that utilizes a custom algorithm to improve power trace routing and clearance on the PCB.
body:
> Merged follow-up: half-width pad-clearance postprocessing continues in #3.

## Summary

- add a whole-board `<autoroutingphase reroute />` driven by a custom `algorithmFn`
- pin [`@tscircuit/power-trace-expander`](https://github.com/tscircuit/power-trace-expander) at commit `9d3d0d9371bdb6abf973426e31f8c9884332f9e4`
- preserve conforming/imported routes, widen collision-free intervals in place, and obstacle-reroute blocked intervals within a 10 mm replacement window
- probe intermediate widths, elastically push lower-width blockers, and use bounded multilayer A* with one/two vias and terminal necking for severe chokes
- remove redundant same-layer via pairs, simplify toward 0/45/90-degree geometry, and transactionally shove lower-width neighbors
- keep routed vias out of pads, repair clustered/duplicate routed vias, and target 0.15 mm power-to-pad spacing while retaining the 0.1 mm hard DRC rule for unavoidable package escapes
- use Flatbush broad-phase indexing with exact trace, pad, via, drill, and board-edge checks
- add solder-mask-covered GND copper pours on both copper layers
- refresh and inspect the broad PCB snapshot

## Solver and debugger

- solver PR: https://github.com/tscircuit/power-trace-expander/pull/3
- step-through debugger: https://power-trace-expander.vercel.app

The debugger has 12 simple and three complex fixtures, including focused routed-via-in-pad, clustered-via, and power-to-pad-clearance cases.

## Measured result

On the production fixture, the 1 mm routes improve as follows using Circuit JSON's length-weighted first-route-point segment-width semantics:

| Metric | Input | Expanded + cleaned |
| --- | ---: | ---: |
| full-width coverage | 1.27% | 87.40% |
| coverage at or above 0.5 mm | 18.13% | 93.37% |
| average width | 0.232 mm | 0.938 mm |
| 5th percentile | 0.150 mm | 0.375 mm |
| 10th percentile | 0.150 mm | 0.750 mm |
| normalized width deficit | 76.76% | 6.16% |

The stricter endpoint-minimum lower bound reaches 86.20% full-width coverage, a 0.934 mm average, and a 6.61% deficit. Cleanup removes seven redundant via pairs (14 vias), normalizes 71 arbitrary-angle segments, and commits five extra-clearance shoves. The 0.25 mm logic routes reach 99.48% full-width coverage.

The isolated upper `P_MOTOR_A` route retains exactly two vias and over 10 mm of bottom copper, with greater than 97% conservative full-width coverage. The short legal neck is longer than before because its via is now outside the connected terminal pad.

## DRC and validation

The complete board render is independently checked with `@tscircuit/checks` and has zero circuit/routing errors with no allowlist. A shape-aware audit also verifies that no autorouted via overlaps any SMT pad. At the preferred 0.15 mm power-to-pad target, only 11 pad/trace pairs remain below target; every one still passes the hard 0.1 mm DRC.

- solver: 32 tests, 390 assertions, typecheck, build, benchmark, and SVG snapshots pass
- board: 23 assertions, full render, DRC, copper-pour geometry, pad-clearance budget, via/pad audit, typecheck, and PCB snapshot pass

title: Add half-width power pad clearance cleanup
description: Add bounded multilayer A rerouting for severe under-width intervals, including onetwo-via routes, endpoint necking, and transactional lower-priority trace shoves, along with debuggable post-route stages for redundant via-pair removal, power-to-pad clearance, and final path simplification.
body:
## Summary

- add bounded multilayer A* rerouting for severe under-width intervals, including one/two-via routes, endpoint necking, and transactional lower-priority trace shoves
- add debuggable post-route stages for redundant via-pair removal, power-to-pad clearance, and final 0/45/90-degree path simplification
- target unrelated-pad edge clearance at half of each power trace's nominal width by default, while accepting the best descending 0.025–0.05 mm tier when the full target cannot fit
- keep via-pair removal at the established 0.15 mm preferred-clearance tier, then let the dedicated clearance pass push the simpler route toward half width; this prevents clearance preservation from bringing redundant excursions back
- preserve the clearance achieved by the pad-spacing stage when the later simplifier rewrites a route
- keep prospective and existing routed vias out of connected pads, enforce drill spacing even on the same net, and relocate invalid vias without touching intentional footprint thermal vias
- retain the 10 mm local reroute bound and Flatbush-backed exact collision checks
- add `onlyConnectionNames` targeting through the solver and autorouter factory

## Debugger and integration

- deployed step-through debugger: https://power-trace-expander.vercel.app
- motor-controller integration: https://github.com/tscircuit/rp2040-motor-controller/pull/3

The debugger catalog has 13 simple and three complex fixtures. Focused cases cover full and constrained half-width clearance, routed-via-in-pad repair, clustered same-net via separation, direct and obstacle-aware via-pair elimination, and clearance shoving. Each scan, clearance tier, candidate, grid search, shove, rollback, and acceptance remains visible through the generic solver debugger.

The production regression now identifies the `R_ISEN_B` ground connection semantically and requires no via within 2 mm of either endpoint. The motor-controller integration separately verifies that the BISEN-side `R_ISEN_B` PCB trace is via-free.

## Measured result

For the captured board's 1 mm routes, Circuit JSON first-route-point width semantics improve from:

| Metric | Before | After |
| --- | ---: | ---: |
| full-width coverage | 1.27% | 87.39% |
| coverage at or above 0.5 mm | 18.13% | 93.57% |
| average width | 0.232 mm | 0.939 mm |
| 5th percentile | 0.150 mm | 0.375 mm |
| 10th percentile | 0.150 mm | 0.750 mm |
| normalized width deficit | 76.76% | 6.09% |

The stricter endpoint-minimum measurement reaches 86.19% full-width coverage, a 0.935 mm average, and a 6.53% normalized deficit. Cleanup removes seven redundant via pairs (14 vias), normalizes 63 arbitrary-angle segments, and commits five extra-clearance shoves.

The clearance histogram measures the same post-expansion geometry immediately before and after cleanup:

| Preferred pad clearance | Before cleanup | After cleanup |
| --- | ---: | ---: |
| 0.15 mm | 25 | 19 |
| 0.25 mm | 58 | 48 |
| 0.30 mm | 84 | 68 |
| 0.40 mm | 97 | 84 |
| 0.50 mm (half width) | 122 | 106 |

Removing the three additional redundant via pairs is a deliberate Pareto improvement over retaining their layer excursions: four more segment measurements remain below the aspirational 0.50 mm tier, while the direct routes remain DRC-clean and all six vias stay eliminated. Dense package escapes still satisfy the board's hard 0.1 mm rule. The via repair pass relocates five routed vias and leaves zero unresolved via violations.

Logic-route full-width coverage reaches 99.48%. A representative run completes in 15.6 seconds with 1.185M solver steps, 8,351 planar-grid attempts, and 51 layer-grid attempts, below the 20-second/4.1M-step regression gates.

The isolated upper `P_MOTOR_A` route retains its two-via lower-layer escape and greater than 97% conservative full-width coverage; its layer-change via remains outside the connected terminal pad.

## Validation

- `bun test` — 33 tests, 403 assertions
- `bun run typecheck`
- `bun run build`
- `bun run benchmark:rp2040`
- `git diff --check`
- solver SVG snapshots refreshed, including the full RP2040 Dual Motor fixture
- full `rp2040-motor-controller` render with `@tscircuit/checks`: no routing errors, no allowlist, no routed-via/pad overlaps, and no vias on the BISEN-side `R_ISEN_B` trace

> Merged follow-up: half-width pad-clearance postprocessing continues in #4.

## Summary

- add bounded multilayer A* rerouting for severe under-width intervals, including one/two-via routes, endpoint necking, and transactional lower-priority trace shoves
- add a debuggable post-route cleanup phase that removes redundant same-layer via pairs and simplifies power paths toward 0/45/90-degree geometry
- keep prospective and existing routed vias out of connected pads, enforce drill spacing even on the same net, and relocate invalid vias without touching intentional footprint thermal vias
- add a configurable preferred power-trace-to-pad clearance (0.15 mm by default), with local octilinear/grid rerouting and clearance-preserving simplification
- retain the 10 mm local reroute bound and Flatbush-backed exact collision checks
- add `onlyConnectionNames` targeting through the solver and autorouter factory

## Debugger and integration

- deployed step-through debugger: https://power-trace-expander.vercel.app
- motor-controller integration: https://github.com/tscircuit/rp2040-motor-controller/pull/2

The debugger catalog has 12 simple and three complex fixtures. Focused cases cover routed-via-in-pad repair, clustered same-net via separation, preferred power-to-pad clearance, direct via-pair elimination, obstacle-aware via-pair detouring, and clearance-shove simplification.

## Measured result

For the captured board's 1 mm routes, Circuit JSON first-route-point width semantics improve from:

| Metric | Before | After |
| --- | ---: | ---: |
| full-width coverage | 1.27% | 87.40% |
| coverage at or above 0.5 mm | 18.13% | 93.37% |
| average width | 0.232 mm | 0.938 mm |
| 5th percentile | 0.150 mm | 0.375 mm |
| 10th percentile | 0.150 mm | 0.750 mm |
| normalized width deficit | 76.76% | 6.16% |

The stricter endpoint-minimum measurement reaches 86.20% full-width coverage, a 0.934 mm average, and a 6.61% normalized deficit. Cleanup removes seven redundant via pairs (14 vias), normalizes 71 arbitrary-angle segments, and commits five extra-clearance shoves.

The via repair pass relocates five routed vias and leaves zero unresolved via violations. The preferred 0.15 mm power-to-pad scan reduces below-target segments from 25 to 19; the remainder are dense package escapes that still satisfy the board's hard 0.1 mm DRC rule.

Logic-route full-width coverage reaches 99.48%. A representative run completes in about 12 seconds with 1.12M solver steps, 8,351 planar-grid attempts, and 51 layer-grid attempts.

## Validation

- `bun test` — 32 tests, 390 assertions
- `bun run typecheck`
- `bun run build`
- `bun run benchmark:rp2040`
- `git diff --check`
- broad solver SVG snapshot refresh, including the full RP2040 Dual Motor fixture
- full `rp2040-motor-controller` render with `@tscircuit/checks`: no routing errors, no allowlist, and no routed-via/pad overlaps

body:
## Summary

- add length-weighted trace-width measurement, a repeatable RP2040 benchmark, and residual bottleneck analysis
- widen clear geometry to the best safe intermediate width instead of only a few coarse candidates
- add a granular elastic solver that gently pushes lower-width blocking traces before falling back to obstacle-aware A*
- canonicalize connection aliases across source-trace names, merged names, PCB ports, traces, pads, and vias so same-net copper is not treated as an obstacle
- add exact capsule, polygon, and circle narrow-phase checks behind the Flatbush broad phase
- repeat productive board passes until copper-area gain falls below 0.1%, with a four-pass safety cap
- remove hot-path full-grid scans, reuse immutable spatial indices, and early-exit collision queries
- add an intermediate-width debugger fixture plus same-net, clearance, geometry, plateau, bottleneck, and performance regressions

## RP2040 Dual Motor result

Metrics use Circuit JSON's first-route-point segment-width semantics and are length weighted.

| 1 mm route metric | Input | Expanded |
| --- | ---: | ---: |
| full-width coverage | 1.27% | 80.02% |
| coverage at or above 0.5 mm | 18.13% | 92.07% |
| average width | 0.232 mm | 0.906 mm |
| 5th percentile | 0.150 mm | 0.350 mm |
| 10th percentile | 0.150 mm | 0.500 mm |
| normalized width deficit | 76.76% | 9.41% |

The stricter endpoint-minimum lower bound reaches 78.06% full-width coverage and a 0.897 mm average. The 0.25 mm routes reach 99.47% full-width coverage. The 1 mm route length increases 5.73%.

The production fixture stops after three passes; the last adds only 0.013% of nominal copper area. A representative local run takes about 9.5–10.2 seconds versus about 23.8 seconds before these changes. The regression caps wall time, solver iterations, and attempted grids.

## DRC behavior

The motor-controller integration reruns `@tscircuit/checks` on the fully rendered board and reports no new routing DRCs beyond the same three pre-existing, explicitly allowlisted same-net ground-via spacing reports at the imported RP2040 boundary.

I also evaluated a more aggressive variant that let under-width power traces push peer power traces. It reached 82.77% full-width coverage, but the full-board DRC exposed different-net contacts, so that strategy is intentionally not included. Route-point transition checks remain conservative enough to match the integrated board DRC.

## Validation

- `bun test` — 22 tests, 120 assertions
- `bun run typecheck`
- `bun run build`
- `bun run format:check`
- `bun run solver:debug:build`
- `bun run benchmark:rp2040`
- `bun run analyze:rp2040`
- `git diff --check`
- RP2040 solver and simplified SVG snapshots refreshed and visually inspected
- complete `rp2040-motor-controller` render with `@tscircuit/checks` routing validation passes using the local package

title: Support Bun Git package imports
description: Changes package entry points to support Bun Git package imports by pointing to the tracked libindex.ts source, allowing consumers to install directly from GitHub without a prebuilt dist directory.
body:
## Summary

- point the package `main`, `module`, `types`, and export map at the tracked `lib/index.ts` source entry
- include `lib` in package contents so Git dependencies do not require a prebuilt `dist` directory
- document a pinned `git+https` package.json dependency and scoped package import

## Why

Git dependencies install the repository source without a generated `dist` directory. The previous package entry points targeted untracked build output, so consumers installing directly from GitHub could not resolve the package. These changes make Bun load the committed TypeScript source directly.

## Impact

Consumers can pin the repository as a `git+https` dependency and import the package normally. Library source and TypeScript configuration remain unchanged.

## Validation

- `bun run typecheck`
- `bun test` (10 passing)
- `bun run build`
- installed commit `468ac6f` into a fresh temporary project using the documented `git+https` dependency form
- imported `DifferentialPairSolver` from `@tscircuit/length-matching-post-process` in that consumer project
- `bun pm pack --dry-run` includes the library TypeScript sources

title: Add no-op differential pair solver scaffold
description: Add the initial tscircuitlength-matching-solver package contract and ESM build, validate complete routed Simple Route JSON and explicit differential-pair constraints, preserve constructor inputs and return structurally independent no-op outputs, document compatibility behavior for deprecated embedded constraints, and add unit coverage and a labeled Cosmos fixture.
body:
## Summary

- add the initial `@tscircuit/length-matching-solver` package contract and ESM build
- validate complete routed Simple Route JSON and explicit differential-pair constraints
- preserve constructor inputs and return structurally independent no-op outputs
- document the compatibility behavior for deprecated embedded constraints
- add unit coverage and a labeled Cosmos fixture

## Why

This establishes the package boundary needed for core to invoke differential-pair post-processing after routing. Version 0.0.1 intentionally preserves route geometry; coordinated length matching remains a later implementation step.

## Impact

Consumers can integrate against a validated, immutable solver API now. The package is not published by this PR; publication and core migration remain tracked separately.

## Related work

- Parent PRD: #1
- Closes #2
- Closes #3
- Provides the package-side coverage required by #7
- Companion core integration: tscircuit/core#2782
- Publication and migration: #8

## Validation

- `bun test` — 7 passed
- `bun run typecheck`
- `bun run build`
- `git diff --check`
